Andrew o'Flynn:
Hi All,


I’m Andy or Andrew O’Flynn. I am Irish, from Dublin, and living in Berlin for 5 years now since joining Zalando. I currently work as a Buyer on one of the Premium Buying teams.


Outside of work I enjoy hanging with my family (I recently became a father), walking my dog, skating with friends or cooking to relieve stress after a long workday!


I have seen a lot of change in my 5 years at Zalando, some redundancies and many restructures. During this time I have seen many ups and downs. I decided to run for the WC as I wanted to be able to contribute in a tangible and positive way to help my fellow colleagues. I have met many amazing people in my time at Zalando and I believe together we can make a difference and make positive change that benefit all employees.
Kristall Thamas:
Hey, my name is Kristall (she/her). I’m the daughter of Afghan refugees, born and raised in Germany.

Growing up, I often felt like I was living between two worlds: my Afghan and my German identity. That experience shaped my motivation to understand different perspectives and to create environments where people with diverse identities feel a sense of belonging.

I studied Gender & Diversity and started my career in HR, driven by the goal to support people and address challenges that impact all of us.

Since joining Zalando in 2018, I’ve worked in Talent Acquisition and later moved into the D&I team. There, I’ve supported various initiatives, especially our ERGs, and collaborated across many teams and business units.

What has always driven me is a simple belief: most of us want the same things. To be treated with respect, fairness, and kindness, and to feel heard.

To deepen my impact, I joined the European Works Council and contributed to strengthening employee representation, including the election of employee representatives to the Supervisory Board.

Now, I want to continue this work and contribute even more and I’m excited to be part of the Global Workforce Community list, as we share the same goal: making work better for everyone.
Talat Anand:
Hi, I’m Talat.

I’m an applied scientist at Zalando, originally from India, and I’ve been living in Berlin for many years now.

Having spent most of those years at Zalando, I’ve come to see both the good and the bad sides of our company. I’ve experienced moments of real support, but also periods that made it painfully clear how much management decisions, as well as company and team culture, can affect people’s lives.

I’ve been there myself.

Around 2020, a major burnout, followed by its classic symptoms of depression, anxiety, and panic disorder, radically changed my perspective on life. It opened my eyes to the importance of work-life balance, of supportive environments, and the fact that mental health and working conditions cannot be separated.

It also made me appreciate the often underappreciated, selfless work of the people in the Works Council, who help shape and strengthen the better side of this company. Having benefited from that work, like so many of us have, I would like to give something back.

My motivation is also deeply rooted in Buddhist thought, especially in the principle that beyond divisions and labels and ideologies, only one thing matters: one must work towards reducing suffering wherever and whenever one encounters it. For me, that means standing for the people I work with, directly and indirectly, standing for my colleagues with empathy, fairness, transparency, and a shared sense of responsibility for the community we are all a part of. Ultimately, it means working toward creating a workplace that guarantees people's dignity as human beings, just as it does their productivity.

That is why I’m running.
Mahmoud Gaballah:
Bridging the Gap: From Advocacy to Action

The Weight of Being Unheard I joined Zalando in 2020 as a Software Engineer, eager to contribute to a company that promised a culture of belonging. As a core member of the Muslim community Network, I spent months advocating for stronger inclusion measures, but found that our Diversity & Inclusion initiatives, while well-intentioned on paper, often lacked the practical frameworks needed to fully support marginalized colleagues.

From May 2022 to April 2025, I served as a substitute member of the Works Council. During this time, I raised critical grievances regarding these issues, but found the structural response from both the company and the Works Council to be insufficient. This experience taught me something vital: we cannot simply wait for the system to correct itself. We have to be the ones to claim our rights.

Building a Bridge: The D&I Committee When I became a full Works Council member in April 2025, I didn't just want a seat at the table; I wanted to ensure the table served its true purpose. I co-founded the Diversity & Inclusion Committee because I refuse to let another colleague feel isolated. My mission is to turn "inclusion" into actual, lived protection by drafting the necessary policies to proactively counter discrimination and ensure equity for all.

Negotiating for Your Dignity I believe a Works Council should be your shield, especially when you are most vulnerable. This is why I stepped into the room to negotiate our Internal Investigation Policy. These principles and values are the foundation of my personal motivation for engaging in these negotiations:

  • The Presumption of Innocence: No one should be judged before the facts are in.

  • Privacy & Professional Integrity: clear guardrails on information usage so that employees are treated as trusted colleagues.

  • The Right to Dedicated Support: I believe no one should ever have to navigate a daunting interview alone.

  • Clarity of Process & Rigorous Oversight: I stand for an environment where everyone has accessible information regarding their rights.

Vigilance Behind the Scenes Much of my daily work takes place in the Personnel Committee, reviewing the documents that shape your careers—from hiring and transfers to terminations. I understand deeply that these are never just "files"—they represent your livelihoods, your families, and your peace of mind.

Because of this, I approach every single document with rigorous due diligence. My responsibility is to ensure that every decision is based on a complete, objective picture rather than just an administrative summary. In the past, whenever legal boundaries allowed, I have made it a priority to seek context directly from the individuals involved. My goal has always been to ensure your perspective is fully represented and considered before any final decision is made, bridging the gap between a paperwork process and its real human impact.

Radical Transparency as a Commitment I believe we must strive for a much higher standard of transparency than what is practiced today. You deserve to understand the processes that affect you,

Operating at the Legal Maximum: We will share every single piece of information, strategy, and progress that we are legally allowed to share with you. While we strictly safeguard private personal data and true business secrets, we will not use inaccurate claims of "confidentiality" as a convenient excuse for silence. We are committed to exhausting the legal limits of communication so you are never left in the dark.

Your Feedback is a Mandate: Radical transparency also fundamentally changes how we treat your voices. The Works Council should not operate in a silo—rarely listening to employees and almost never acting on your feedback. We are putting an end to that. Your feedback shouldn't just be politely heard; it must be treated as a clear mandate and a direct order from the people who elected us.

Why Global Workforce Community? I joined this list of colleagues because we represent the true Zalando—every country, every ERG community, and every department. As we look toward the 2026 Works Council elections, I am not here to make legally impossible promises. I am here to offer a relentless commitment to your support. I’ve been the advocate in the room defending your interests, and I am ready to continue being that voice. Let’s move from reacting to leading.
Ahmed Abbas:
Stop Negotiating Our Rights Away

Ten years at one company gives you a long memory. I’ve seen our leadership styles change, and I’ve watched our core values shift from being genuinely "lived" to being merely "tokenized" on presentation slides.

As an Engineering Manager, I have a unique perspective: having been part of the operational implementation of our process, I have seen the structural disconnect between what we aspire to be and the reality employees experience on the ground

Through my network, I’ve seen too many cases where employees, especially those who are international or unfamiliar with German employment laws, fall through the cracks only because they didn't know their rights. When you don't know the rules, you are vulnerable. And unfortunately, the Works Council has not always been the necessary uncompromising shield it needs to be to protect those people.

I’ve also spent time studying the philosophical and societal roots of German labor laws. These laws weren't written to be polite suggestions. They exist specifically to prevent an unbalanced, optimized-for-output-only culture. And I know that if these rights aren't asserted by competent representatives, they stay on paper while the reality on the ground remains purely output-driven.

I am joining the GWC list because I believe in competence, honesty, and holding the line.

My Core Principle: We do not negotiate down on statutory rights. Those are the floor. We use our legal leverage to negotiate for the extras - the better social plans, the fair transitions, and the genuine protections you deserve. But a right is only an "idea" until someone has the spine to assert it.

From my relationship with this team, I know we possess the experience and diplomatic attitude to find a balance of interest with the employer - but we will never do it by compromising your baseline rights.

Outside of work: You’ll find me recharging by walking in parks and nature—sometimes enjoying the quiet alone, and sometimes with friends and family.
Wasim Sawalha:
Hi, I am Wasim Sawalha from Jordan , and I have been part of Zalando since 2018. Throughout these years, I have grown with the organization and today serve as Head of ERP Retail Solutions. But most important, I am simply a colleague, someone who understands the challenges, ambitions, and everyday realities we all share.


I joined the Global Workforce Community list because I believe our diversity is our strength,

I believe representation is not just a responsibility, it is a commitment. A commitment to listen. A commitment to speak up.


I deeply believe in justice and equity for all. Not as abstract words, but as lived values. My motto is simple: “Justice for one is justice for all.” When one person is treated unfairly, it concerns all of us. When one voice is ignored, we all lose strength. But when we stand together, we build a culture of trust, dignity, and real inclusion.

I will always advocate for open and respectful dialogue, fair decisions, and a supportive environment where each of us can thrive.

Together, we can shape a workplace built on trust, respect, and equal opportunity and we can ensure that every colleague feels valued, and empowered.
Andrea Sella:
I joined Zalando in May 2020, and since then, I have enjoyed tackling the complex engineering challenges inherent to our business. After several years in the Partner Tech department - where I helped partners integrate price, stock, and product - I moved to PRSB and now Cloud Infrastructure organisations. There, I work alongside talented teams to provide tools for the wider software engineering community.


I am a firm believer in taking full ownership and responsibility for my actions (and inactions) on a daily basis; it is through our mistakes that we grow into better humans, both personally and professionally. Because organizations and individuals are constantly evolving, we can only truly thrive by acknowledging this ever-shifting nature. I joined this list because such transitions must be navigated with both mind and heart. Only by ensuring just and fair treatment for our colleagues can we guarantee their and their families’ financial security.


My pronouns are they/them and you can address me with any variant of Andre*. If you would like to have a chat about what I do on a daily basis at work, feel free to book a slot.
Kim Ozor:
Hi everyone,


I’m Kim! Originally from the cursed state of Florida, I’ve been living in Europe since around 2012, and moved to Berlin to work at Zalando in 2017. I’m queer, chronically-ill, autistic, and disabled, and I have 2 dogs George (the blind one) and Milly (the elderly one) and a silly, mischievous cat named Alistair - all rescues.

At Zalando, I’ve been a fashion product developer in Private Labels, and a works council member since 2022. Sadly last autumn, my whole job family was discontinued. For years my colleagues and I speculated anxiously in whispers on when computers, AI, and outsourcing would take our jobs, with every new development inching us closer and closer to our expiration date. I think that’s unfortunately a common feeling in this time we live in. We see the world changing around us and feel helpless to stop it, helpless to influence it.

It’s more important now than ever to unite as workers, to band together in community, and to know who your friends are - who will stand up for you, and who will protect you when your job is on the chopping block. I joined Global Workforce Community because it’s the list with heart, the courage to do what’s right even when it’s hard, and the compassion to care for each and every worker.

I like being a Works Council member because I like helping people. Over the past 4 years, I’ve helped many of you. I’m that phone-a-friend you call up whether you have a question or a crisis. My problem solving approach is that we try everything, every path- and when we run out of paths, we make our own path.

My biggest wins personally over the past term have been on individual cases - stopping the employer from stealing back someone’s bonus, extending the probation period for someone who was on long-term sick leave (they’re now a full employee by the way :)), establishing a fully dog-free allergy safe-zone in the office so that colleagues can work safely without ending up in the hospital. As Vice-chair of the petitions committee, I’ve been your champion petition facilitator (and at times petition ghost-writer). As chair of the D&I working group, I’ve led us in renegotiating the compliance policy, and investigating discriminatory practices

Look, the world kinda really sucks right now. But if we unite and work together effectively, we can make the change that we want to see, in the workplace and in the world at large. Global Workforce Community is the list that will take us there.
Daniel Calderon:
Hello, I'm Daniel Calderón Villela, a Honduran-American-German born in Tegucigalpa, Honduras. I've moved around a lot, living in 7 different countries of the Americas including 18 years in the United States, having short stints in Taiwan and China and now 7.5 years in Germany. Whilst in Germany I've been to 23 of the 27 EU countries. Through those travels I’ve aspired to find the similarities across the world's cultures while understanding the differences.


I've been at Zalando for 7.5+ years, always on the same contract having been hired to work as a Privacy Engineer on Data Protection, a discipline in which I was specialized in with a masters from Carnegie Mellon University. I am currently assigned as an Application Security Engineer in Information Security. Privacy is a human right to me.


I joined the 1st Works Council term as a substitute in Feb 2021, and became more active in the 2nd Works Council term from 2022-2026. Along with many partial contributions, I list a selected list of some of what I've championed and focused on this term:

  • Taking on individual cases of employee hardship

  • Protecting the Employee-friendly aspects of Hybrid Work, like championing the rejection of an Employer’s proposal to introduce an attendance dashboard, which was rejected.

  • Bringing privacy mindset to the Works Council in reviewing potentially privacy-invasive new IT, like negotiating the regulated use of the monitoring tool Palo Alto Cortex XDR (which alerts for abnormal behavior on company devices), and negotiating the introduction of the monitoring tool zScaler (which serves as a VPN replacement for ivanti)

  • Championing a more inclusive workplace by re-negotiating with the Employer the anti-discrimination processes at Zalando

  • Championing the introduction of a Petitions Process to simplify for employees the submission of legally valid employee petitions

  • Negotiating and Delivering the Electronic Working Time Recording Works Agreements and Annexes

  • Negotiating to make the major reorganizations in 2023 & 2026 proceed more justly


I've learned a lot about organizing through this work, coming to see that organizing is needed to capture the democratic voice of the employees, & that the institutional power of the Works Council is needed to bring that voice to the table with Management. I want a works council that better reflects this need.


I also see a need for a Works Council that truly reflects the current global workforce we have, and that champions its priorities. With nearly 1/3 of our workforce from Germany, and 2/3 not, we need a works council that works for every employee, wherever they may come from. A valid work contract defines participation and not any other criteria, so someone not from this country has a right to be included, and to feel that they belong and that someone like them is representing them. I want to help build a Works Council that succeeds in this representation.


I speak English by training and US immersion, hablo español por mi crianza inmersa en el español latinoamericano, und ich versuche jetzt, mein Deutsch zu verbessern!
Míriam Sabaté:
Hello everyone,


I’m Míriam Sabaté (she/her), originally from Barcelona, Spain.

I’ve lived in Berlin for 11 years and have been working at Zalando since 2018, always in the same team: Ocean & Air Transports Team, navigating the complexities of logistics for Private Labels and Wholesale.


This experience has given me a front-row seat to the volatility of the industry. The reality is that the current geopolitical instability, the challenges of the retail and logistics industries, and the AI bubble affect Zalando and pose a risk to our jobs.


However, my years at Zalando have taught me that we shouldn't stay passive in the face of uncertainty. During my two and a half years as a zBeat Ambassador for the Transports Solutions Team, I learned how to drive bottom-up change, listening to my colleagues and their concerns and ensuring our voices were heard when it mattered the most. I want to take the same proactive energy to the Works Council.


I’m excited to be part of the Global Workforce Community list and run for the WoCo:

  • To fight in your corner to ensure German labour law is respected and we advance our rights whenever possible.

  • To support everyone who needs us and ensure zero tolerance for discrimination.

  • To finally bring transparency to the WoCo processes, be receptive to your feedback and implement accountability and continuous improvement measures.

I would be honoured to represent you and empower you in the next Works Council term.


On a personal level, I have a cat and I’m currently fostering another. I love Karaoke, crafts, sci-fi, fantasy and horror in any format and organising get togethers.


Feel free to reach out to me or book a slot with me to discuss any topic that concerns you or simply give us feedback.
Ahmed Ebaid:
Hi everyone, I’m Ahmed. Since 2018, I’ve helped build and secure our digital infrastructure as a Network and Security Engineer. While my professional focus is on protecting complex systems, my true passion has always been connecting people. I believe our greatest strength comes from how we mentor one another, share knowledge, and lift each other up.


For a long time, the Works Council wasn’t my plan. But the difficult moments many of us have experienced over the past few years changed my perspective. Standing in solidarity with our Employee Resource Groups opened my eyes to the real pressures and marginalization many colleagues face. It reinforced an important lesson: our communities have authentic power, but goodwill alone is not enough. Sustainable fairness requires structure. When support depends only on permission rather than clear policy, people can easily be left unprotected.


Through hardship, we must forge strength. Instead of waiting for change, we must actively help build systems that ensure fairness and equity for everyone. Your voices should not simply be collected - they should shape the decisions that affect our everyday working lives.


That is why I am running with the Global Workforce Community. Our team brings together colleagues from many backgrounds, roles, and experiences, united by a shared commitment to fairness, transparency, and strong representation. My role has always been to secure vulnerable systems — now I want to help secure our collective rights through genuine goodwill, collective intelligence, and responsibility.


Beyond work, I find my balance in poetry, drawing, and practicing Taekwondo. I am a passionate supporter of Al Ahly FC and enjoy sharing the warmth of Mediterranean cuisine. I value open hearts, honest dialogue, and thoughtful, strategic conversations. If you'd like to connect, please feel free to reach out.
Mosheera Salah:
Hello everyone,

My name is Mosheera Salah. I joined Zalando in September 2023 as the Lead of ZTF PMO, bringing with me a 21-year career built on the belief that project management is as much about people as it is about processes.

My path has taken me from Head of PM Center of Excellence to overseeing Global Operations as director for a major French firm.

In 2023, I sought a new horizon and made the move from Egypt to Germany to embrace the dynamic challenges of the tech landscape.

My 'True North' has always been Fairness. In a fast-paced corporate world, I see my role as more than just a strategist; I am an advocate.

I strive to be the voice for those unheard and a steady hand when ethical ground needs to be held. To me, Diversity and Inclusion aren't just KPIs—they are the bedrock of a culture where every individual feels a genuine sense of belonging.

This is why I joined the Global Workforce Community "GWC " List ; their commitment to fostering an inclusive, high-performing, and equitable workplace perfectly matches my own values and my mission to drive authentic change here at Zalando.

When I’m not driving projects, I trade my keyboard for a pottery wheel or an oil brush. Art teaches me the same lesson I bring to the GWC: that with patience, precision, and a bit of soul, you can transform raw materials into something truly meaningful.
Wilfredo Manrique:
Hi! My name is Wilfredo. You can call me Wil. I'm a tropically-grown eccentric from Caracas, Venezuela ☀️🌴. I’ve been a Software Engineer at Zalando since 2016 (back when our motto was "Re-imagine Fashion for the Good of All").

Arun Yogeshwaran:
Hello everyone, I’m Arun. I joined Zalando in March 2025 as a Software Engineer, having moved from India. In this short time, I’ve seen how essential our international community is and how much strength we draw from it.


Although I’m new to the Works Council related topics, I’m eager to learn quickly and gain the knowledge needed to be an effective advocate.


Like many of my colleagues, I believe our workplace must guarantee fairness and protection not only when things are going well, but especially in challenging times.


My commitment to the Global Workforce Community is driven by a desire to champion transparency and fairness, by helping to build an environment where every employee understands their rights and where decisions are made openly and clearly, reflecting Global Workforce Community’s call to end the era of filtered information.


I also want to ensure that the Works Council truly reflects and advocates for the priorities of our global workforce. With two-thirds of our colleagues being non-German, it is crucial that every employee, regardless of background, feels seen, represented, and protected.


I’m ready to step up and contribute my energy to our collective well-being. Feel free to reach out - I value honest dialogue and thoughtful, strategic conversations.
Agustina Cadenas:
Hello eveyone! I am Agustina (she/her). I was born in Argentina, Buenos Aires.
I joined Zalando 1 year ago, and I am part of the Size & Fit team.

I decided to join the Work Force Community list after several conversations about our shared values. In a multinational company like ours, where we share our daily space with colleagues from all over the world, I believe our Works Council should reflect that reality.

I want to be part of the list because I want to ensure real representation for all nationalities, backgrounds, religions and cultures. It is essential that we bring diverse perspectives to the table, especially for recent and sad geopolitical events that are happening around the world. We cannot ignore what is happening.

We all deserve to be treated equally and to have our voices represented.

On a more personal note, I am a woman of maaany hobbies. I’m a fashion designer, which means I’m obsessed with fabrics and sewing. I also like to swimming, making jewelry, or deep into some DIY craft project 😀


I’m also trying to be active in the community. You can find me at soli parties, events, or demonstrations. So, if you ever need someone to join you at an event or want to organize something together — reach out to me :)
Mathis Hagen:
Hi, I’m Mathis (he/him)!


I am from Berlin and simply never left. Been with Zalando since 2017, first as a working student and full time after 2020, always in IT Security. I have come to love interacting with the people I worked with and met here and believe that this kind of international community is quite rare and precious, especially in Germany.

I’d argue that a strong WoCo is necessary for the wellbeing of not just the employees but the company at large. Because it is us employees with our expertise that make this company run well and Zalando has been alienating its workforce quite a bit, first with program 23 and now with a new wave of layoffs.

Having experienced a couple reorganisations in the past, I have little hope that Zalando changes its approach and improves the communication towards employees leading up to a reorg. But we can change the WoCo and the way it handles these situations and the transparency it displays toward us employees.

I’d like for the WoCo to host more informational events. ⅔ of our colleagues are not from Germany and even the ones that are will likely not be fully familiar with their rights as employees in Germany specifically. I am very much included in that. But since the Woco receives legal training and education on these rights, I’d want the new WoCo to do a better job at sharing this knowledge. The most pressing example is dismissal protection of course and it is where we have seen some educational content, but it feels like that was mainly one member of the WoCo, not the whole body supporting it.


I’d love to see a WoCo that is more proactive and communicates better with its constituency and I think the Global Workforce Community is the best list to achieve this.
Iberê Floriano Rios:
I am Iberê, originally from Brazil, I joined Zalando in 2016, worked in customer care tech for many years and recently moved into ZEOS.

I worked as an engineering manager for 4 years and during that time, I tried to lead my team with full transparency.

I'm a supporter of the open salaries initiative and hope for a future where we can understand decision making by leadership and which ways the collective rights are negotiated.

I support the list goals of bringing as much transparency as possible to the Works Council negotiations and to work collectively for employees rights, not only through individual cases.
Valeriia Shcherbakova:
I’m Valeriia, originally from Kyiv 🇺🇦, and I’ve been part of the Lounge iOS team for almost four years now. During that time, I’ve worked in three different teams and setups, which gave me a pretty real understanding of how different working environments can feel — when they function well, and when they don’t.

I’m running for the Works Council because I care about how we experience our work every day — not just what we deliver.

Work-life balance, psychological safety, and feeling respected shouldn’t depend on luck, a specific manager, or how comfortable someone feels speaking up. They should be part of how we operate as a company. I know from personal experience how vulnerable it can feel to go through longer sick leave or difficult periods — especially when the signals around support aren’t always clear. That’s something I believe we can and should improve.

To me, the Works Council is about making sure people feel protected, heard, and treated fairly — not only when things are going well, but especially when they’re not.

Outside of work, I’m a big yoga fan, a proud dog mom to Oggy (our unofficial BHS dog-friendly inspector 🐶), and yes — probably wearing pink more often than necessary 💗.

I believe we can build an environment that is ambitious and high-performing, but also kind, clear, and supportive. If elected, I’ll focus on practical improvements, transparent communication, and making sure people don’t have to navigate difficult moments
Anna Khoreva:
I'm Anna, originally from Russia, and I've been living in Germany since 2012. I joined Zalando in 2024 as the Head of Applied Science for the Size & Fit team.

My commitment to the working council is driven by a deep belief in our collective power. It's essential to me that we move beyond mere representation and actively ensure that every colleague's perspective is truly heard and translated into effective action.

Global Workforce Community is a vibrant group of individuals—a true reflection of the many countries, backgrounds, and experiences that make up Zalando. I believe this diversity isn't just a characteristic; it's our greatest asset. By celebrating our differences and committing to open collaboration, we're building an environment that feels more inclusive, drives better ideas, and makes us all stronger.
Alberto Perbellini:
Hi everyone! I'm Alberto Perbellini, originally from Italy, and I've been part of Zalando since June 2019 as a Senior Project Manager.

I've always believed that if you want things to change, you have to show up and make it happen. Sitting on the sidelines and hoping for the best has never been my style and honestly, it's a lesson I want to pass on to my kids too. That's what brought me to the Community: the conviction that active participation is the only real way to shape the environment around us, whether at work, at home, or in society.

Fairness and transparency aren't abstract ideals to me they're the foundation of any place worth working at. I want to be part of a Works Council that earns the trust of every colleague by being honest, open, and genuinely accountable.

Outside of work, you'll find me with a book in hand, out on a football pitch, or somewhere in the mountains recharging.
Alexander Kraskov:
Hey, my name is Alex, originally from Russia, came to Berlin in 2009 as a part of the Spätaussideler family, and started my career in Zalando in 2012. Currently work as an engineering manager in a feature team.


As a foreigner who went through the entire process of integration, as a worker who observed Zalando growing from a startup to a big international company, as a former individual contributor and as a manager with 6+ years of experience, I would like to be able to help our fellow colleagues navigating the difficult waters of work life in Germany.
Katyanna Moura:
Hello! I’m Katyanna (:

I moved from Natal 🇧🇷, the "Bride of the Sun", to Berlin 8 years ago and have been a software engineer at Zalando for 4 years. I value fairness and transparency and want to help ensure fair treatment, real opportunities for career progression, and the most open communication possible.
Carla Figueiredo:
Hi, I’m Carla. Originally from Portugal, I’ve been a part of Zalando for nearly 4 years.

My work is driven by a deep belief in fairness and justice. For me, these aren't just words, they are the standard we should all expect in our workplace and life.

The Works Council is incredibly close to my heart because I believe every employee deserves a voice that is respected and protected.
Swastika Mishra:
My name is Swastika Mishra, and I was born in India. I joined Zalando last July as a Senior Data Engineer.

I am actively involved in the Global Workforce Community, a diverse network of colleagues from different countries, backgrounds, and experiences. This community reflects the richness of our global workforce, and we strongly believe that diversity is not only something to celebrate but also a source of strength. By working together and embracing our differences, we create a more inclusive, collaborative, and innovative environment.
José Badilla:
Hi! My name is José Badilla and I’m originally from Panama City, Panama. However, I’ve been living in Germany for a bit over 7 years.


I moved to Germany for work because the labor laws here are designed to treat you like a human being first and a worker second. In some other places, it can feel like you could be fired without warning just to save the company a few bucks. Here, the strict employee protections mean we should have real job security.


This peace of mind and stability is invaluable and should be defended by a strong WoCo. I am joining this list so I can be a participant in any actions that ensure we respect the dignity of every employee in the company, especially when it comes to restructuring and layoffs. I also look forward to ensuring our WoCo accurately represents and portrays the needs of the employee.
Felipe Marin:
I am Felipe Marin, I have been working for Zalando for the past 8 years, working in IT Compliance, BI and Data topics.

I am here because I really enjoyed the discussions with the Global Workforce Community members and I am very in line with the cause regarding employees rights and fairness.

Looking forward to meeting you all!
Katja Eikenberg:
Hi everyone! I'm Katja Eikenberg, and I've been a part of Zalando for over six years. My heart has always been in logistics, and I've had the pleasure of working across various parts of the company in that field with amazing people.

I joined Zalando's Connected Retail in 2019. Inspired by the team, we rapidly onboarded store owners during the pandemic. Later, I led a Delivery Solutions team, managing final-mile deliveries across Nordics, Spain, France, Italy, UK, and Ireland. A late 2024 reorg shifted me to First/Mid-mile Process Excellence. Despite my last-mile focus, I embraced the change. My role's sudden redundancy, coupled with witnessing colleague disrespect, motivated my decision to run for the Works Council.

Starting October 2024 I became an active part of the ZWN and took over responsibilities as CORE member being Pillar Lead for Advocacy. I led events like IWD25, Pink October initiatives and the Elephant in the room. I see the women in Zalando so active in the ZWN and we have an ambition to create and foster opportunities for the empowerment of women at Zalando and beyond. It makes me proud that I joined this community and supported this ERG.

Outside of Zalando, you can find me spreading joy as a Zumba instructor! It's my purpose to bring happiness, relief, and a workout that uplifts our souls. This is the reason I am also part of the peer power program in Zalando, giving a class once a month to all my colleagues.

The WoCo and My Candidacy: The Motivation
As employees, we can feel vulnerable and often don't know our rights, which can differ so much between countries. I want to ensure all colleagues feel safe and supported, especially when hard times hit. I'm running because I genuinely want to see things change and bring more transparency for every single one of us. I'm ready to take this journey now and stand up for what's right, always keeping my colleagues' best interests in mind.

  • Safeguarding Your Serenity: Stability and dignity are invaluable, especially during restructurings and layoffs. I want to be an active participant in ensuring we respect every employee.

  • Wisdom is Strength: I am committed to learning the complexities of labor laws and translating that employee representation information into clear, actionable knowledge for all of us.

  • Less Talk, More Action: I want to make the WoCo more approachable and engaging, connecting it with those who currently feel disconnected.

I love getting really good at things and making a difference, and I want to use that to make sure everyone has a respectful and good place to work.
Casper Rutz:
Hey folks! I’m Casper (he/him or they/them) and I’ve been working at Zalando since 2021 as a Software Developer in Partner Tech (Zalando Direct). I’m from the USA – I was born and raised in Chicago, but lived in Utah a few years before escaping to Portland, Oregon. My partner and I moved to Berlin in 2021 with my partner and our two cats.

Why I’m running for the Works Council is a bit of a personal story, but one that I think it’s important to share. I am queer, and while I grew up in an awesome and largely queer-friendly city, I moved across the country to go to university in a part of the country that was deeply LGBT-unfriendly, where verbal abuse, systematic oppression, or even physical violence were common. I spent my entire time there terrified to be myself, and after I left I promised myself I would never hide who I am again and would do everything in my power to make sure no one has to go through something similar, no matter if they are queer or not.

I’ve tried my best over the years to make good on that promise. I’ve been volunteering with the Pride@Zalando - LGBTQI+ ERG since 2022, most recently as a CORE member and Pillar co-lead. There’s a chance you might recognize me from a few videos and panel talks I’ve been featured in along the way. I admire my colleagues in the ERG and their mission to build acceptance and diversity at Zalando, and I’ve been able to connect with and learn from many people I otherwise wouldn’t ever see in my day-to-day.

Over the years, it’s become clear to me that more solidarity among Zalandos would uplift all of us. Together we are stronger, and what helps the most vulnerable among us helps us all. I am proud to be standing in solidarity with the others on the Global Workforce Community list running for the Works Council. I look forward to being able to represent you.
Sylvia Lu:
Hi everyone, I am Sylvia.

I grew up in a Chinese American immigrant family in New York. That shaped my curiosity, empathy, and openness to different perspectives. For me, diversity is not an abstract idea; it is a lived experience.

I have worked across public and private sectors, in highly structured organisations with strict governance and in fast-growing, scaling environments. That range gives me insight into how organisations operate and how change can be communicated and implemented effectively. I understand business realities and value clarity, accountability and thoughtful execution in decision-making.

I have been with Zalando for 5 years in Talent Acquisition. During this time, the company has experienced growth, restructures, and leadership transitions. Change is inevitable, but how it is managed makes all the difference. Transparency, fairness, psychological safety and support are essential for people to perform at their best, knowing they are heard, seen, and supported.

For me, genuine representation means employees understand their rights, decisions are made thoughtfully with input from all levels, and concerns can be raised safely. In a globally diverse organisation like ours, equal voice and support should apply to everyone across all levels and backgrounds. Every voice counts and deserves representation.

Business priorities matter, but so does human impact. We are more than roles or headcount. Each of us brings responsibility, ambition, and a life beyond work.

I advocate for a workplace where business goals and people align, and decisions reflect care and accountability. People should feel respected, informed, and have opportunities to grow.

I would like to contribute my experience to strengthen a healthy work environment where people and business goals work together effectively. If this resonates with you, let’s connect to drive positive change.
Hesham Abdelhamid:
I came from Egypt to work as a software engineer in Zalando in 2018. I passed through multiple re-orgs and changed roles & teams several times. I am currently an engineering manager in the content department.

I think that the Works Council plays an important role in shaping and informing decisions both on individual & overall levels to ensure fairness especially during transitional and challenging times like the covid and post-covid era or the ongoing AI shift with its impacts. I also believe the WoCo should represent the diversity and different backgrounds of the people working in Zalando and should aim for protecting minorities from biases.
Lara Laila\tGärber:
My name is Lara Laila Gärber (she/her) and I am Palestinian-German. I joined Zalando in the summer of 2025 as a Senior Diversity and Inclusion Manager.

Before that, I spent eight years at Anheuser-Busch InBev, the world’s largest brewer, where I worked across several functions and later led Diversity and Inclusion for Zone Europe and the Middle East. Two initiatives I am particularly proud of are extending the parental leave policy to more than 13,500 colleagues across 20 countries, and establishing the company’s first anti-harassment and anti-discrimination campaign, launched internally and in collaboration with nine other companies, including competitors.

I care deeply about fairness and justice, which is why I am running with the Global Workforce Community. In today’s political climate, globally and in Germany, we are seeing heightened levels of racism, repression and fear. At moments like this, it becomes essential for us to unite.

Outside of work, I dedicate time to initiatives that advance justice and freedom. Over the past years, I have moderated 17 panels and conducted 53 interviews with renowned organizers, activists, artists and lawyers working toward justice.

One lesson that has stayed with me is that each of us can contribute something different, sometimes even something small, but together the collective becomes powerful.

I look forward to meeting many of you and would be happy to connect.
Shubham Bansal:
Hello Lovely People,


I am Shubham Bansal, originally from India. I have been a resident of Germany since 2011 and am now a German citizen.


My first tenure at Zalando was from 2014 to 2018. During this period, I witnessed the company's growth firsthand. However, I also personally experienced certain indirect challenges, or "indifferences," and observed how they could impede an individual's professional development within a large corporate structure. Ultimately, these issues led to my earlier decision to leave the company.


In 2022, I returned to Zalando, taking on the role of Engineering Manager in the core platform team. Being in people leadership has given me insight into the various ups and downs, conflicts, and opinions that engineers face due to multiple challenges. I believe that my past experiences, combined with my current leadership perspective, uniquely position me to contribute significant value by elevating the company's foundation, which is inherently built on its people to the next level.
Daniela\tNigro:
Hello, Daniela Nigro here! A cheerful Italian by origin, who travelled across 40+ countries, and lived in 5 of them in the last 15years.

In this journey I connected with incredible human beings and experienced many different cultures, realising that real inclusion goes through the acknowledgment that we are all unique (rather than different), along with respect and empathy.

I value open communication, I champion fairness, equity, and I firmly believe that honest conversations, build trust, drive impact and contribute to creating those safe spaces, also to disagree!

I'm enthusiastic about people, cultures, languages, and food. Too boring? I love cooking, eating ( I am italian after all!), Improvisation theatre, dancing, skiing, and connecting people (did I say people already?)

I joined Zalando over 4 years ago, in the Data Foundation dept, and beyond my role as Principal in the Product team, you'll find me actively building connections and fostering communities, whether it's organizing events for the Italian community, or helping to bring together the data community by organizing the Zalando Data Connect, which successfully launched last year (and will repeat)! Another wide community I’ve recently committed to supporting is the “Community of Employees,” by serving as an active substitute member in the Electoral Board to assist with organizing this Works Council election.

My decision to run for the WoCo is driven by a commitment to support my colleagues and amplify the voices of those who may be hesitant to speak up for various reasons. It also comes as a response to the difficult and ever changing global geopolitical situations, its impact on job market circumstances, and the uncertainties this bring to my fellows employees. I firmly believe that we shouldn’t just “be thankful to have A job”, rather we should keep performing and enjoying our work life balance, without a pending threat that things can change for one moment to the other!

Things can surely change, but timely information allows risk management, like in every well managed project!

I joined the Global Workforce Community because it aligns with these principles, guided by genuine intent, passion, expertise, and a dedication to advocating for employees, learning from experience, and ensuring reliable support for the workforce as a whole, no matter where one is from, or which affinity groups they belong!

For that to happen, we need you tag along!;) Come meet me and I’ll be happy to share more!
Frankie Thyer:
Hi! I’m Frankie (they/them), and I’ve been working at Zalando since 2021 as a software engineer, mostly focusing on accessibility and other diversity and inclusion topics. You might recognize me from my work with the Pride and Neurodiversity Employee Resource Groups (ERG’s), where I served as a core group member for 2 years. You might also have seen me on local Berlin stages performing as a drag king. I’m also a voting member of the Electoral Board, working to ensure a fair election, and bringing my experience to improve accessibility where possible and to prevent trans people from being outed by the election process.


In my experience, the most valuable thing Zalando has offered me is the opportunity to connect deeply with incredible people from all over the world. A colleague I met via the Pride ERG is about to be best man at my upcoming wedding! A few years ago, the ERG’s and D&I team helped me feel safe enough to come out as trans nonbinary at work.


I learned the hard way that coming out at Zalando at that time wasn’t as safe and easy as I was led to believe. When things went wrong for me, the ERG’s could empathize and relate, but they lacked enough power to help. The Works Council, however, had real power to take action and put some things right.


While I treasure all the connections I have made at Zalando, I’ve also witnessed deep structural problems and systems that continue to fail us and punish those who don’t fit a narrow template. Although the ERG’s are a wonderful tool for community building, I have seen the limits of what they have the power to accomplish to protect their members and make change.


Everyone deserves to be treated with fairness and equal respect. I cannot in good conscience sit by and watch my colleagues continue to struggle without taking action myself to prevent it and to make sure it does not happen again.


I am a member of the Global Workforce Community because it gathers people from all over the world with varying perspectives and shared values who have the courage and compassion to show solidarity and stand up for what’s right, even when it’s not easy or straightforward. I look forward to serving you!
Allam Abdel:
I believe ultimately in people, that's why I am here, so that we collectively as employees at Zalando make sure that we as people are put first whether as employees or customers. As of these hard times of the machine taking over humans we must come together to make sure as people that our value is determined fundamentally not by the amount of capital we generate nor the magnitude of production we deliver, but rather by the uniqueness of the decisions we make based on real empathy, emotions, and true human connection with people like us; by our ability to make decisions that put human lives, human values and prosperity first as the ultimate purpose before any other purpose, such as the concentration of power and wealth.


My name is Allam, which is my last name and what I like to be called, however on paper and due to bureaucracy in my home country Egypt, it's Mohamed Abdelkhalek.


I will be honored to represent you in the works Council committee of 2026. Let's come together in these challenging times to make sure that AI will not take over our jobs today, our children's intelligence tomorrow, and eventually humanity.


A little about me: I was born and grew up in a small coastal town by the red sea in Saudi Arabia called Yanbu. However I spent a similar amount of my life as a teenager and adult in the historical huge city of Cairo capital of Egypt that is culturally and socially rich, it greatly defined my character and refined my personality. And here I am for the past five rich years in the international city of Berlin, exploring the culture, from tech to techno, and from donor to art.
Bassma Elrafie:
Hey everyone! I'm Bassma (which, fun fact, means "A smile" in Arabic 😊). I'm an Egyptian 🇪🇬 who's called Berlin 🇩🇪 home for 11 awesome years and been part of the Zalando since 2017.


I'm a Principal Tech Consultant in Zalando Tech Foundation, but let's be real ; my 15+ years in banking and e-commerce have really been about being a problem-solving ninja, innovation cheerleader, and the ultimate translator between tech and business. Basically, I make sure the job gets done (and funded!).


You know me from the Zalando SE works council. I've been a substitute member since 2022, soaking up legal knowledge and dedicating energy to the health committee to champion employee well-being. Plot twist: That means I come with zero ramp-up time and already have my priority list set for where we need urgent improvements.


This time, I'm aiming for a permanent seat because I'm ready to turn up the volume and dedicate all my energy to implementing the changes we need!


Want the rest of the story? Keep scrolling... 😉


I'm a proud mom to two awesome kids (13 and 6). I've navigated a career change, maternity leave, and even an employment gap. As a parent and a woman in tech, I've got the real-world playbook for what it takes to ensure every employee can enjoy well-being and pursue their career dreams without life's stages being roadblocks. I love mentoring and actively do it on various free platforms. My latest obsession? Customized gifting, think snow globes, tote bags, fun pens, and jewelry!
Veda Maas:
Hello! I am Veda Maas!


For the past nine years, I have been an integral part of Zalando Private Labels, serving as a dedicated Quality Specialist since 2017. My career in quality assurance extends over 17 years, during which I have traveled extensively across numerous countries and visited countless factories. This unique experience has given me a deep, firsthand understanding of the profound and often troubling disparities in the treatment of workers between what we term the "1st world" and the "3rd world." I have witnessed the impact of business decisions on the lives of people globally, which has fundamentally shaped my core values.


My background is rooted in South Africa, where I grew up during the final, turbulent years of Apartheid. While I benefited from the privilege of being a white South African, this upbringing instilled a fierce sense of responsibility in me. I very quickly realized that it is not enough to simply have privilege—it is my solemn duty to actively use that privilege as a tool to give back, to advocate for the voiceless, and to fight against systemic injustice. Growing up in that environment cultivated an enduring hatred of unfairness, injustice, and mistreatment of any human being.


This foundational moral compass is why I became involved in employee representation. I was part of the first Electoral Board at Zalando and have since served as a substitute on the Works Council (WoCo). I was heavily involved in the crucial Project Nova restructure, where I dedicated myself to negotiating better severance packages and terms for the affected employees. However, since those negotiations, I have grown increasingly disillusioned with how certain matters have been handled, feeling a sense of stagnation and a drift from the council's core mission. I've also become increasingly aware of what I perceive as a culture of superficiality and performatism, which is often characteristic of a rapidly evolving city like Berlin. This disillusionment is a primary motivator for me to step forward now, seeking a direct mandate to refocus our efforts.


I am known for being a very straightforward, direct person. I operate with and strive for absolute honesty and transparency in all my interactions, and I consider these to be the non-negotiable, basic foundations for any effective Works Council.


My philosophy is simple and uncompromising: Our FIRST and ONLY mandate is to stand up and represent the employees—nothing more and nothing less. Our singular focus must be on the rights, welfare, and representation of every single person who works here. If elected, I will tirelessly work to ensure the WoCo operates with this clarity and conviction at all times.


On a personal note, I moved here to Berlin with my husband and our four beloved rescued dogs, who keep me grounded and remind me daily of the importance of care and loyalty. I look forward to the opportunity to bring my deep experience, unwavering integrity, and passionate commitment to fighting for what is right, to serve you on the Works Council.
Lucía Morales:
Hi, I’m Lucía (she/her). I’m from Spain and I’ve been working at Zalando since 2019. I started as a CuCa Specialist and my current role is Operations Specialist, so I’ve seen the company from different angles and realities.

Being involved in collective spaces has always been part of who I am. Between 2020 and 2024, I served as a member of the Works Council in CuCa International. It was a meaningful experience. We pushed for better standards, clearer protections. I was also a member of the Pride ERG in 2024, because representation and safe spaces inside the company matters to me just as much as formal agreements do.

I’ve supported colleagues in conflicts, in uncertainty, in moments when they felt small against a big system. And what I’ve learned is this: most people need someone who listens, someone who explains their options, someone who stands next to them in the room.

I’m running with Global Workforce Community, a diverse list of colleagues from different countries, backgrounds, and experiences. We reflect the reality of our workforce — and we believe that diversity is not only something to celebrate, but something that makes us stronger when we act together.
Ammad Nasir:
I am Ammad Nasir, born in Pakistan, raised in Islamabad, Copenhagen, London, Amsterdam and Berlin.


Academically, I am MBA (Master in Business Administration - Project Management) from Preston College London (United Kingdom), and MA. IMIS (International Management and Information Systems) from FH Südwestfalen, Germany.


With over 17 years of expertise in strategic procurement, contract negotiation, and supply chain management, I have a proven track record of delivering value across the fashion, automotive, industrial engineering, and telecommunications sectors. Since joining Zalando in October 2021, I have held pivotal roles within the Logistics organization. Currently, as a member of the Tender and Market Activities (TMA) team, my role is responsible for end-to-end Last Mile sourcing and contract management across different Zalando markets.


Working councils (WoCo) have pivotal roles in any big organisation in Germany. Myself as a member of the WoCo, I am sure that I will be strongly advocating for:


  1. Driving Radical Transparency in organisational decision making

  2. Championing Diversity & Inclusion in day to day working environment

  3. Protecting and Developing Employee Rights

  4. Professional & Personal Development Programs for employees


To pursue the collective goals, in the upcoming WoCo elections for 2026, I am part of the Global Workforce Community. As the name reflects, our Global Workforce Community reflects our dear and respectable colleagues from diverse nations, cultures, backgrounds, and experiences, yet we all are committed at large to the aforementioned goals.
Edgar Goncalves:
Hey all!

My name is Edgar. I’m originally from Portugal and have been with Zalando for nearly eight years, bridging the gap between Systems and Software Engineering.

I am a firm believer in workers' rights. Over the years, I’ve seen firsthand how the Works Council is more than just a participant - it is a fundamental tool for leveraging our interests with the employer. I’m proud to be part of a list composed of kind, competent people from diverse backgrounds. Together, we offer the varied perspectives necessary for thoughtful decision-making and effective action.

Feel free to reach out! As a Mental Health First Aider 🫶, I’m always here to support you with work-related concerns, but I’m also just as happy to chat about video games or board games.

Looking forward to connecting!
Sabine Hopmann Lopez:
Hi everyone, I’m Sabine.


My background is a mix of cultures—born and raised in Nicaragua to a Nicaraguan mother and a German father. Since moving to Europe in 2009, I’ve lived across various German and European cities before settling in Berlin with my husband and two children. I joined Zalando in 2021 as a Senior Project Manager in Intralogistics and transitioned to ZEOS in 2023 as a Senior Integration Manager.


I chose this list because of its genuine diversity and its focus on concrete initiatives over buzzwords. A perfect example is our push for reskilling programs. Technology changes fast, but an employee’s institutional knowledge is irreplaceable. We want to ensure that instead of losing talent, we provide the path for colleagues to pivot into new roles.


While these are proposals and not yet guarantees, they represent exactly what this list stands for: real actions that protect our future.
Larry Almeida:
Hi! I’m Larry.

I’ve been a Software Engineer at Zalando since 2018. Over the last 7+ years, I’ve worked across a variety of teams and topics - from Site Reliability at Lounge to Sustainability and D&I. I’ve seen our culture from many angles, and I deeply care about the people who make this company run.

Why I’m here:

I’m driven by mastery and adding value. I want to understand the labor laws, regulations, nuances around employer employee relationships and the Works Council so I can ensure they are applied effectively for myself and others.

  • Transparency over Fluff: I want to make the WoCo more relatable and engaging for those of us who currently feel disconnected from it.

  • Knowledge as Power: I want to translate employee representation complexities into actionable information for all of us.

  • Effective Negotiation: I’m here to learn the mechanics of negotiation to ensure our collective voice is heard clearly.
Adrian Schiopu:
I am Adrian, born and raised in Romania and spent most of my life working abroad in highly international and multicultural environments.


I have joined Zalando in 2020 and ever since I’m working as a System Engineer within Zalando Workplace.
I am happy to be here and to support our initiatives in making a positive difference in Zalando.
Especially close to my heart are the initiatives towards more transparency and equal opportunities as well as a reskilling program so much needed nowadays in this ever changing world.
Hakkim Alavudeen:
Hello all,


I’m Hakkim and I'm originally from Cape Town, South Africa. I have been with Zalando since 2020 as Head of Engineering, mainly in the Customer Facing Applications sphere.

Over the past two years, I have come to appreciate how essential fair, transparent, and well-governed processes are within our company. During this period, my employment was terminated three times.


On 28 January 2026, the Berlin Labour Court ruled that all three terminations were invalid and ordered my reinstatement under unchanged contractual conditions. This experience reinforced my belief in the importance of strong institutional safeguards and accountability.


I am interested in contributing to the Works Council to help uphold its core functions to the highest standards:

• Safeguarding fairness in employment processes, incl. restructurings and performance procedures.

• Ensuring that employees who raise concerns are heard and treated with integrity and are not retaliated against.

• Promoting transparency and accountability in decision-making.

• Supporting constructive and solution-oriented dialogue between employees and management.


Outside of work, you’ll most likely find me hiking or underwater — I’m an avid scuba diver and never happier than when I’m somewhere in nature. I bring the same energy to everything I do: stay calm, go deep, and don’t come up until the job is done.
Thorbjoern Knappe:
Hello everyone,


Well, I'm not quite sure how to start this, but I think my name is a good beginning. So my name is Thorbjörn, though only my mom calls me that, and since the pronunciation can be a bit tricky, I go by my nickname TB.


I've been with Zalando since 2011, working in IT across various departments over the years, starting in Internal IT, moving through Platform Engineering, and now having the privilege to work in the Infrastructure Services team.

IT has been my passion from a very early age, and as they say, I have been fortunate enough to turn my hobby into my profession.


I live with my family here in Berlin, the city where I was born and raised.

With young children at home, I can honestly say that my personal hobbies often take a backseat these days. However, I'm already enjoying sharing my love for building blocks and board games with the kids… though motorcycle riding will have to wait a bit longer :)


To be frank, I used to view voting and candidates as something "obligatory that just had to be done." But now, with trusted colleagues by my side who took the time to explain what this is really all about, I am completely convinced that this can be something truly positive if we all work together as a community. Rather than just complaining at the end of the day, I want to take a stand and actually help make a difference. That's why I agreed to participate, and well, here I am.


Thank you for taking the time to read my introduction, and please feel free to reach out if you have any questions.


All the best to you all!
Abdelrahman Saleh:
Hi, I’m Abdelrahman. Originally from Egypt, I lived my childhood and grew up across other countries like Syria, Lebanon, Libya, and Saudi Arabia before joining Zalando as a Data Engineer in 2022.


I moved to Germany with high expectations of fairness, transparency and freedom. My time here has taught me a vital lesson: these rights are not simply 'given', instead they are maintained and protected by those willing to stand up for them. I joined the Global Workforce Community to be a voice for my colleagues and to ensure no one has to fight for their rights alone.


I believe our strength lies in unity. By looking beyond our individual needs and cooperating across our diverse backgrounds, we can build a workplace that truly benefits everyone.
Antonios Maronikolakis:
My name is Antonios, I am originally from Greece and I joined Zalando in October 2023 after finishing my PhD in Natural Language Processing. I am currently an Applied Scientist working in Search & Reco, and previously on the Zalando Assistant.


I am a member of the Global Workforce Community because I believe in times of difficulty and uncertainty, both inside Zalando and globally, workers need to be more organised than ever. In order for the Works Council to actually be a trusted representative of Zalando employees, we need transparency, fairness and commitment to the struggle and the needs of workers. At the Global Workforce Community list we are uniting and standing together to fight for our collective rights.


Apart from work, you can find me reading, talking endlessly about football players from “back in my day” and going to demonstrations. If you are interested in any of the above or you want to have a chat, I would love to connect!
Marta Skarzynska:
Hi! I'm Marta. I’m originally from Poland (80s Solidarity!) and have been with Zalando for four years (including a one-year maternity leave). I moved to Germany nine years ago and am still enjoying the expat life—now in 'mum mode'.

Transparency, equality, and respect are at the top of my list, and I believe there’s plenty of room for us to grow in these areas. Driven by empathy and curiosity, I want to be a positive force for change within WoCo and Zalando’s broader work culture.

On the personal side, I’m a total foodie, a jigsaw puzzle lover, an occasional traveler, and a cardio-endorphin enthusiast. I'd describe myself as a short-term realist and a long-term aspiring optimist. Looking forward to connecting with you all!
Selin Kozaneli:
Hi everyone!

My name is Selin Kozaneli.

As a Berlin native with Turkish roots and a proud mother of two, I bring both a local heart and a multicultural perspective to our team. Having spent over 8 years at Zalando, currently serving as a Senior Event Manager, I have seen our company grow and evolve from the inside out.

I am running for the Works Council because I want to use my experience in organization and communication to ensure every voice is heard. My goal is to advocate for a fair, transparent workplace and to champion a better work-life balance for all of us. I am very glad to have joined the Global Workforce Community, as I believe my deep roots in the company, combined with my passion for people, will help me represent our interests effectively and make Zalando an even better place to work ❤️.
Henrik Lenk:
Hi everyone,


I'm Henrik, working as a Junior Manager in the Customs Export Team since March 2020.


I’m supporting the customs clearance processes & the operational business for exports and returns to / from non EU countries (CH,UK,NO).


What motivated me to be part of the Works council?


During the last 2 years I was often disappointed how Zalando is dealing with the ongoing world events and not taking a clear stance against clear injustice and favoring certain groups over others.

I strongly oppose such behaviour and support equal rights for every employee.


That’s why I’d like to support employees with the same view as a works council member.
Patrick Keenan:
Hallo everyone,


my name is Patrick Keenan and I’ve been working at Zalando since December 2017; I am Irish but grew up in the UK, and here I am a Merchandise Planner currently working in the Streetwear Sneakers team; when I’m not at work neck-deep in Excel or trying to contend with Anaplan, you can probably find me posting memes or playing video games.


I love working at Zalando, and one of the main reasons is the people; when I come to the office I always find myself surrounded by great people from all over the world, people from all kinds of backgrounds and experiences - I have made lifelong friends here, and I hope to continue making even more.


Although I have always been passionate about fairness and standing up for people (you may have seen me in various Slidos asking difficult questions), I have decided to take a more active role in our workplace by standing with the Global Workforce Community, a diverse group of people that I believe represent the best of what our Zalando community can be - I want to empower and stand up for my fellow colleagues from every part of Zalando, because ultimately what makes Zalando a success is the people.


I believe in fairness and transparency for all no matter what, and though I love working at Zalando it is a universal truth that companies do not always operate with their employees’ best interests at heart, but you can rest assured that I will do whatever it takes to speak out and stand up for what’s fair.
Hala Al Kuwatly:
I am Hala Al Kuwatly, a Syrian who moved to Germany more than 10 years ago. I joined Zalando in 2023 as an Applied Science Manager, leading the Applied science team in customer care.


My professional experience sits at the intersection of Software Engineering and Applied Science. I initially worked as a software engineer before pursuing my Master's degree in AI at the Technical University of Munich, which led me to specialize in AI and data science.


On a personal note, I am a relatively new mother to a very spirited and opinionated 18-month-old girl :)!
